title: The Perron Farmhouse — Threshold
description:
Rhode Island, 1971. You have come to investigate the farmhouse that broke Ed and Lorraine Warren.

The house exhales cold air through its open door, though no window is broken, no vent is open. The Warren files you carry describe Bathsheba Sherman — a woman hanged from the oak tree behind this property in 1863 after reportedly sacrificing her infant to the Devil. The land was cursed before the foundation was ever laid.

Your flashlight flickers once, then steadies. The entrance hall smells of burnt tallow and something older — iron, copper, rot. Somewhere deep in the house, a music box begins to play. Lorraine Warren herself fled from this exact sound.

You hear a child whisper '{playerName}' from upstairs.

decisions.text: Enter the chapel corridor — Ed Warren left notes about 'the black heart of the house' here

consequence.text:
The corridor breathes. You are not imagining it — the walls contract and expand in slow, rhythmic pulses. The portraits lining the hall are all of Bathsheba: different decades, different frames, but always the same cold black eyes. One has been slashed by fingernails from the inside of the canvas.

At the end of the corridor, a crucifix hangs upside down. Beneath it, someone has drawn a goat's head in what the Warrens' files confirm is not paint.

The music box stops. The silence is worse.

decisions.text: Descend to the root cellar — Roger Perron described 'something living down there'

consequence.text:
Twelve steps. With each one, a degree colder. The root cellar is exactly as Carolyn Perron described in her testimony: the stone altar in the northeast corner, the iron shackles bolted to the wall, the unmistakable smell of old blood in the earth.

But the Warrens' file said the altar was destroyed in 1975. It stands here whole, unmarked, as if it was built yesterday. As if it has been waiting.

Something carved into the stone reads: SHE DID NOT LEAVE. SHE NEVER LEAVES.
consequence.evidence: Perron cellar altar — active and intact

decisions.text: Investigate the study — Lorraine's notes mention 'documents that should have been burned'

consequence.text:
The study is untouched since 1973. Dust thick enough to hold shape. A single candle burns on the desk — impossible, since you are the first person through that door. On the desk, a leather folder stamped WARREN CASE FILE #19 — CLASSIFIED.

You open it. The first photograph is of this room. Taken now. But dated 1973. You are standing in the photograph with your back to the camera.

The candle flame bends toward you, away from the draft.

title: The Desecrated Chapel
description:
The chapel was consecrated in 1842. Bathsheba Sherman unconsecrated it six years later.

An inverted cross dominates the far wall — not hung upside down, but grown that way, the wood warped by something internal. Each pew faces inward instead of the altar, as though the congregation has been forced to kneel facing each other for decades. Seven black candles burn in a deliberate pattern that Ed Warren identified in his notes as a summoning array.

On the altar: a baptismal font filled with black water. The reflection inside shows a room this chapel does not contain.

The Warrens documented that Bathsheba used this place to conduct blood rites on the second Saturday of every month. Today is a Saturday.

decisions.text: Search beneath the pews — Lorraine recorded a vision of 'something hidden here in God's name'

consequence.text:
Under the third pew from the front, wrapped in a cloth of undyed linen and sealed with a wax cross, you find what the Warrens left behind.

A hand-carved cross of rowan wood, blessed by Father Achampong at St. Michael's in 1973 during the Warren investigation. Lorraine wrote in her diary: 'If we do not survive this, whoever comes next will need this more than we did.'

The moment you close your fingers around it, the seven candles gutter — not out, but dim, as though something has noticed a threat.
consequence.evidence: Warren-blessed rowan cross — left for the next investigator

decisions.text: Approach the inverted cross and pray the Lord's Prayer

consequence.text:
You begin. 'Our Father, who art in—'

The cross bleeds from the wood. Not runs — bleeds, rhythmically, as a wound bleeds. The baptismal font boils. A voice beneath the floorboards answers every phrase of the prayer in an inversion: a language that is the Lord's Prayer spoken in reverse, in a voice that is neither male nor female, neither living nor entirely dead.

When you finish, every candle snuffs at once. In the darkness, something cold and deliberate presses the shape of a hand against your sternum from inside your ribcage. It squeezes, once, to show you it can.
consequence.evidence: Demonic response to sacred prayer — Chapel, Saturday rite

decisions.text: Examine the summoning array — document it as Ed Warren did in 1971

consequence.text:
Seven candles. Seven animals represented in ash between them — goat, raven, cat, serpent, wolf, spider, and one you cannot identify. The array forms a shape Ed Warren annotated as a 'demonic aperture' — a literal door held open through sustained ritual.

As you finish sketching it, the aperture responds. The air in the center darkens. A smell like hot copper and burned hair. You see a hand reaching from the darkness between the candles, fingernails black and too long.

You step back. The hand stops. It waits.
consequence.evidence: Active summoning array — seven-point demonic aperture

title: The Root Cellar
description:
Three investigators entered this cellar before Ed Warren. None stayed more than four minutes. Warren himself lasted eleven minutes on his first descent and said afterward: 'There are places on earth that have been given to something that isn't God. This cellar is one of them.'

The shackles on the north wall are worn smooth — worn smooth from the inside. The altar stone is scarred with Latin phrases carved in haste, each one a different handwriting, a different era. The most recent set of carvings is dated 1975. The Warrens' last visit. But below it, in fresh cuts that still hold dust: an inscription dated three days ago.

You are not the first to come here recently.

decisions.text: Read the inscriptions — all of them, from oldest to newest

consequence.text:
The oldest inscription is a prayer of binding. The next is a desperate plea. The third is a warning: DO NOT SPEAK HER NAME. The fourth, in Latin: 'She feeds on witness.' The fifth is in a language you don't know but your body does — your hands begin to shake. The sixth is from the Warrens: case notes, a binding prayer, the date 1975.

The seventh — dated three days ago — is in your own handwriting. It begins with your name: '{playerName}.' A confession of every sin you have committed, in precise detail, ending with: 'I should not have come here.'

Your hands are moving. You are writing the eighth inscription now.
consequence.evidence: Altar inscriptions — eight generations of investigators

decisions.text: Search the old medicine cabinet on the east wall

consequence.text:
It belonged to the investigators who came in 1975. Inside: a half-used bottle of prescription sedatives — Valium, still sealed in their original blister pack — and a handwritten note taped to the label:

'When the walls speak and the floor breathes, these will quiet the noise enough to think clearly. Take no more than two. God bless you. — L.W.'

Lorraine Warren's initials. She left these for whoever came after her. She knew someone would.

decisions.text: Approach the shackled wall and examine what's behind it

consequence.text:
The shackles are bolted into the stone with iron rings. Between them, scratched deep over decades: names. Forty-seven names of people the Warrens could never account for — visitors who came to this property and were never reported missing because no one remembered them visiting.

Among the forty-seven, three names are scratched differently. More recently. In a childlike hand.

One of the three names is '{playerName}.'

The shackles rattle once — measured, deliberate — as though a greeting.
consequence.evidence: The wall of the disappeared — 47 names, including yours

title: The Children's Ward
description:
The Perron children slept in these rooms.

Andrea, Nancy, Christine, Cindy, and April — five daughters who reported waking to the same sight every night: a woman standing in the corner, smelling of dead flowers and rot, her feet hovering two inches above the floor. April, the youngest, called her 'Mama.' The Warrens never corrected her. They weren't certain she was wrong.

The rooms are full of toys that belong to no child who ever lived here. A porcelain doll sits in the center of the largest room. It was not here when you entered the corridor. It arrived while you were walking.

Its head is slightly turned toward you.

decisions.text: Examine the porcelain doll — the Warrens documented a similar object in the Hodgson case

consequence.text:
The doll's eyes are glass but they follow. The Warrens documented this exact phenomenon with Annabelle — a Raggedy Ann doll that a medium confirmed was inhabited by a demonic spirit masquerading as a dead child. This one has the same quality of patient, watching intelligence.

You pick it up. The temperature in your hand drops to near-zero. A child's voice from inside the doll: 'You smell like the others. You smell like food.'

You set it down. Its head was facing left when you picked it up. Now it faces right. You did not turn it.
consequence.evidence: Inhabited doll — demonic intelligence confirmed via temperature and movement

decisions.text: Search the wardrobe — Carolyn Perron described a 'hollow sound' from inside

consequence.text:
The wardrobe is full of children's clothes in sizes that span decades. At the back, behind the hanging garments, is a small door. Behind the small door is a room that should not exist — a space too wide for the house's exterior to contain.

The room holds nothing but a rocking chair and a single photograph nailed to the wall. The photograph is of Ed and Lorraine Warren standing in this exact room. They are holding hands. Their eyes are closed. They are praying. Behind them in the photograph, just visible in shadow, is a shape that looks like a woman standing with her arms at unnatural angles.

On the chair: a child's journal. The last entry reads: 'She says we will all be here forever. I don't want to be here forever. Please come get us.'
consequence.evidence: Hidden room — Warren photograph, child journal recovered

decisions.text: Open the window and attempt to signal outside — you need confirmation you are still connected to the real world

consequence.text:
The window opens. The outside is wrong.

The oak tree in the yard — the one Bathsheba was hanged from — is the only thing moving in a landscape otherwise frozen as a photograph. Its branches move in a wind you cannot feel. Its shape in the dark is unmistakably the shape of a person with arms raised.

Something scratches the outside of the window glass from below, as though climbing. The scratch marks are fingernails: deliberate, patient, climbing upward. They reach the sill. They stop.

You close the window. The scratching continues from inside.

On the sill, left behind by whoever opened this window last, is a tarnished brass key engraved with a confessional cross.
consequence.evidence: External paranormal activity — Bathsheba's oak, active scratching

title: The Confessional Alcove
description:
The confessional was installed in 1855 by a priest named Father Dumont who moved into the house after the Shermans and lasted four months before being found catatonic on the front lawn. His written account, recovered by the Warrens, describes hearing confessions from something that was not human — confessions of acts committed before humanity existed, sins without names in any living language.

The confessional screen is carved with faces. They are not the faces of saints.

You hear someone inside. Breathing with a rhythm that does not match any living creature's need for oxygen. Slow. Deliberate. Patient beyond measure.

decisions.text: Sit in the confessor's seat and listen — as the Warrens' demonologist instructed, document everything

consequence.text:
The voice begins without preamble. In Latin first, then English, then in a language older than either. It confesses the murders. The century of manipulations. The seven children taken. The three investigators driven to permanent madness. Carolyn Perron's possession, described from the inside — what it felt like to inhabit a living woman, to move her hands against her will, to speak with her voice while she screamed in silence behind her own eyes.

Then: 'Now I will confess yours.'

It knows things only you know. Events no one witnessed. It describes them in precise, affectless detail. It is not guessing. It has always known.

You cannot stand. Your legs will not respond. You are going to be here for a very long time.
consequence.evidence: Confession recording — demonic autobiography, personal surveillance confirmed

decisions.text: Speak through the screen — demand to know what Bathsheba wants

consequence.text:
A long silence. Then, quietly: 'I want what was taken from me.'

'What was taken?'

'My name spoken with fear. My power acknowledged. My house respected.' A pause. 'And one more soul to join the others in the walls.'

You ask whose soul. The silence that follows is not empty — it is full of something that knows the answer and is choosing not to give it as a gift.

Then: 'You already know.'

The screen between you fractures down the center. On the other side, where a human figure should be, there is nothing. Just darkness in the exact shape of a person.
consequence.evidence: Direct communication with Bathsheba — nature and intent documented

decisions.text: Leave immediately — the Warrens' protocol states you must not engage the entity alone

consequence.text:
You back away. The confessional does not want you to leave — you feel this as a physical pressure against your chest, a hand pushing inward from nothing. But you push through it.

As you reach the door, the voice from the confessional calls after you. Not threatening. Almost sorrowful:

'You will come back. They always come back to confess. You have so many things you haven't told anyone.' 

The door seals the sound behind you. In the silence of the corridor, you realize you cannot remember the last twenty minutes. The recording in your pocket, which you did not knowingly start, shows twenty-two minutes of audio.

title: The Séance Room
description:
The Warrens conducted their séance here on November 1st, 1974. The session lasted eleven minutes. The recordings of those eleven minutes are sealed in the Vatican Secret Archives at Lorraine Warren's personal request. She said only: 'No one should hear what came through that night.'

The round table still bears the scorch marks from the candles. The mirror on the far wall is covered with a black cloth — covered by Warren, who refused to remove it, who said what was in the mirror was no longer just a reflection.

Something about this room makes your body unwilling to cross the threshold. Not fear — your body knows something your mind does not yet. It is trying to protect you.

decisions.text: Sit at the séance table and attempt contact — document everything the Warrens could not

consequence.text:
You are not alone at the table. You understand this the moment you sit — the chair across from you has weight in it that light cannot account for. The air is occupied.

A voice rises through the table itself, through the wood: 'Say my name.'

You know you should not. But your mouth is already opening. The name tastes like copper and old perfume.

The scorch marks on the table rearrange. They spell something in a language you can read — a binding contract, your signature already at the bottom in a handwriting that is yours but from a hand you have never used.

You signed this before you were born.
consequence.evidence: Séance contact — pre-existing contract recovered, demonic signature confirmed

decisions.text: Remove the cloth from the mirror — what the Warrens hid must be documented

consequence.text:
The cloth falls. The mirror does not reflect the room.

It shows a corridor you have not walked yet. At the end of the corridor is a door. Behind the door is a brightness that hurts to look at directly — not because it is holy, but because it is the opposite of holy made visible.

In the mirror's corridor, moving toward you from the far end, is a figure. A woman. Her feet do not touch the floor. Her eyes have been replaced with something black and liquid. She moves slowly. She has all the time she needs.

As she reaches the mirror's surface from the inside, she places her palms flat against the glass. The mirror is warm. The glass bulges outward.
consequence.evidence: Warren's mirror — active portal confirmed, entity visible

decisions.text: Recite the Lord's Prayer and demand the room be cleared — the Warrens always opened with this

consequence.text:
The prayer lands in the room like a stone in still water. Ripples. Something recoils — not destroyed, not banished, but pushed back, hissing with a sound like steam under pressure.

The candle stubs on the séance table light themselves in response: not warmly, not in worship, but in defiance. As though announcing: still here. Still watching. Not afraid of your prayer, only inconvenienced.

But in that moment of inconvenience, you feel the room breathe clean air for the first time. Just once. Then the pressure returns. It is patient.
consequence.evidence: Demonic response to exorcism prayer — entity confirmed, partially bounded

title: The Passage Below
description:
Ed Warren documented the passage in his private journal but never published the information. His note reads: 'The passage beneath the north foundation does not appear on any building record. It was not built. It arrived. The soil around it has been disturbed continuously for at least three centuries.'

The passage is narrow enough that you must turn sideways in places. The stone walls are wet — not from groundwater, from something warmer. The passage slopes downward at an angle that the house's foundation cannot account for. It is deeper than the building above it should allow.

Where the light cannot reach, you hear movement. Not random. Purposeful.

decisions.text: Follow the passage to its end — the Warrens never did, it may hold the key to the entity's origin

consequence.text:
The passage ends in a chamber that predates the house by centuries. The walls are natural stone, but they are carved — every surface covered in petroglyphs and symbols that archaeologists would date to the 17th century. They depict a ritual. The same ritual, repeated hundreds of times with slight variations, each version showing a different woman in the central role. The final version shows Bathsheba Sherman by unmistakable iconography.

The one carved after hers shows no human figure. Just the shape of something that wears the house like a body.

At the chamber's center: a stone basin filled with black water that does not reflect the ceiling. It reflects something far above it — or far below.
consequence.evidence: Pre-colonial ritual chamber — entity origin site, 400-year ritual cycle confirmed

decisions.text: Map the passage walls — the symbols may reveal how to end this

consequence.text:
The symbols coalesce into a pattern. The Warrens' demonologist, Father Javier, identified this pattern in a 1973 letter to Ed: a 'tethering glyph,' meaning the entity is bound to a specific physical point and can only be permanently expelled by destroying that point's sanctity or sanctifying it beyond its capacity to hold darkness.

The physical point is the altar in the root cellar.

You understand now why the Warrens never fully succeeded. They tried to bind her outward. To close the door. But the door cannot be closed from outside the room.
consequence.evidence: Passage glyph mapping — expulsion method identified

decisions.text: Return upstairs — the passage is too exposed

consequence.text:
You turn back. The passage has lengthened. What took you four minutes to walk takes twenty minutes to retrace. The walls shift imperceptibly — by fractions of inches, but enough. The passage is breathing, contracting and expanding in a cycle too slow for eyes to track but not too slow for your nervous system.

By the time you find the cellar stairs, you have been in the passage for forty minutes. You were only gone eleven. The Warrens noted this temporal distortion in their files. Ed called it 'the house digesting a visitor.'

The stairs creak under your weight. But you didn't step on them yet.
consequence.evidence: Temporal distortion in sub-basement passage — active spatial manipulation

title: The Investigation Archive
description:
Whoever came here after the Warrens tried to do what investigators do: they documented everything. The archive room holds four decades of evidence that no one ever filed, published, or retrieved.

Boxes of photographs. Reel-to-reel audio tapes labeled in careful handwriting. Notebooks from at least six different people. A corkboard covered in strings connecting clippings, symbols, and names — and at the center of the board, circled in red ink, a single date: today's date.

Someone knew you were coming. Someone has been waiting a long time to show you this room.

The door locks behind you without a sound.

decisions.text: Study the corkboard — understand what you're actually facing

consequence.text:
The corkboard tells the story of every investigator who entered this house after the Warrens. Fourteen people. The strings show how each of them moved through the house, what they found, when they stopped finding anything — when they stopped.

Eleven of the fourteen have no exit record. No discharge from the property. They are simply present on the board until they aren't.

The newest string is red. It traces the rooms you have already visited. Someone added it recently — the ink is still slightly wet. The string leads from this room to a final pin at the center of the board: THE FINAL CHAMBER. YOU WILL GO THERE. THIS IS NOT A PREDICTION.

The door unlocks behind you.
consequence.evidence: Investigation archive — 14 prior investigators, 11 unaccounted for

decisions.text: Listen to one of the audio recordings — evidence of EVP may be here

consequence.text:
The first tape is labeled WARREN SESSION 7 — DO NOT PLAY ALONE. You play it alone.

The recording begins with Ed Warren's voice, clear and steady, leading a blessing of the room. Then Lorraine: 'Ed. Ed, don't look at the— Ed.' Then nothing for two minutes. Then a voice that is neither of them: a low, measured voice speaking in a language that sounds like Latin reversed, speaking for nine minutes without stopping.

You understand none of the words. But your body understands. Halfway through the tape, you realize you have been crying since it began. Not from sadness. From recognition. Some part of you has heard this before.
consequence.evidence: Warren Session 7 audio — unknown language, physiological response confirmed

decisions.text: Find and read the Warrens' case notes — their conclusions are the only reliable guide

consequence.text:
The Warrens' notebook is in the third box. Ed's handwriting is precise, clinical. His final entry from this house: 'Bathsheba Sherman was a vessel, not a source. What used her is still here. Still using. The house is not the haunting — the house is the body. The entity is the soul inside it. You cannot exorcise a soul from its own body. You can only ask it to leave. You can only make leaving seem preferable to staying.'

Lorraine's addendum, in smaller handwriting: 'It will not leave willingly. Give it no choice. Give it something it fears more than remaining. We brought the cross. We brought the prayer. We left both here for the one who comes after us. God keep you.'

Behind the notebook, wrapped in a cloth marked with a red cross: a vial of holy water, sealed in wax. Lorraine's label reads: 'Blessed by Father Achampong. Carry this.'
consequence.evidence: Warren case notes — entity classification, expulsion methodology

title: The Corridor of the Possessed
description:
The Warren files document eleven confirmed possessions in this house across forty years. Not all of them were Carolyn Perron. Some were visitors. Some were investigators. One was a priest who came to perform an exorcism and needed one of his own performed before he could leave.

The corridor walls show the evidence: dents from hands at head height, scratches at floor level from fingernails. The doors on either side bear the marks of people trying to exit rooms they could not leave.

The temperature drops here. Not the cold of stone and absence of heat — a directed cold, the cold of something watching, the cold of a gaze.

At the far end of the corridor, a figure stands. It has your silhouette. When you raise your hand, it raises a hand. But it is three seconds ahead of you.

decisions.text: Confront your reflection — the Warrens described this as a 'displacement phenomenon'

consequence.text:
You walk toward it. It walks toward you. The distance between you does not decrease at the rate it should.

At ten feet, you can see its face. Your face. But older. The eyes wrong — not the color, but the quality of what looks through them. It has been wearing your face for longer than you have been alive.

At five feet, it speaks. Your voice, your cadence, but the words are not yours: 'She has made room for you. There are worse things than becoming part of this house. The ones in the walls have each other.'

You reach for it. Your hand passes through. You are alone in the corridor. But you can still hear your own voice, from somewhere inside the walls, finishing a sentence you don't remember starting.
consequence.evidence: Displacement phenomenon — doppelganger contact, pre-possession warning

decisions.text: Open one of the scarred doors and document the room inside

consequence.text:
The room was a bedroom. The bed is overturned. The window glass is broken outward. The carpet is scratched in concentric circles around a point in the center of the room where a person once stood for a very long time.

On the wall, written in a hand that grew increasingly uncontrolled as it descended — calm at the top, frantic at the bottom, then calm again, a different calm, a surrendered calm: the entire text of the Roman Rite of Exorcism. Every word. All forty-three pages, compressed into a single wall, tiny and precise, in a handwriting that matches the photographs of the priest from 1979.

At the bottom, below the last word of the rite: 'It didn't work. Nothing works. But I think I understand why, now. She doesn't fear God. She fears being forgotten.'
consequence.evidence: 1979 exorcism transcript — failure analysis, entity vulnerability identified

decisions.text: Run — get to the final chamber before the entity consolidates

consequence.text:
You run. The corridor extends. The doors multiply. Your reflection stays exactly ten feet behind you regardless of your speed — it is not running. It is walking. And the gap is not changing.

You stop. It stops.

In the silence, you hear the house telling you something — not in words but in the particular quality of its silence, the way a room sounds when everyone in it has been listening to you for a long time.

You have been running in the wrong direction. The chamber is not at the end of the corridor. It is beneath you. It has always been beneath you. The floor gives slightly under your next step, like a drum being struck from below.
consequence.evidence: Spatial manipulation confirmed — entity actively guides movement

title: The Final Chamber — Bathsheba
description:
The chamber is below everything. Below the passage, below the root cellar, below the foundation. It should not exist and yet here it is: circular, stone-walled, lit by no source you can identify.

Bathsheba Sherman stands at the center. Not her ghost, not a vision — she is here. Hanged in 1863 and here in 1971 and here now. Beautiful in the way that wrong things are beautiful. Her feet hover two inches above the stone. Her eyes are the color of the black water in the séance mirror.

She says your name — '{playerName}' — the way a person says the name of someone they have been patient about for a very long time.

'I have been waiting since before you were born,' she says. 'I have been waiting since before your parents were born. In this house I am God. In this house I have always been God. Kneel.'

decisions.text: Raise the Warren's rowan cross and speak the Prayer of Exorcism — give her no choice

consequence.text:
The cross burns hot in your hand. She sees it and the beauty leaves her face — what remains is older and angrier than anything that has ever been beautiful.

'Where did you find—'

You begin. 'I exorcise thee, every unclean spirit, every power of darkness, every incursion of the infernal enemy—'

She screams. The sound is the house itself screaming — the walls crack, the stone weeps, something massive and old tears against a bond it cannot break. She charges toward you and cannot close the distance. The cross holds a perimeter she cannot cross.

You do not stop. The full prayer. Every word. As the final phrase leaves your mouth, the chamber fills with a sound like a lock turning in a door the size of the world. Bathsheba Sherman is drawn downward into the stone. Into whatever waits below.

The lights come on. All of them. Every window in the house above shows morning.

Lorraine Warren's cross lies on the cold stone floor. You leave it where it is. Someone else may need it.
consequence.evidence: Bathsheba Sherman — bound and expelled, Warren rite successful

decisions.text: Present all your evidence and invoke the binding ritual — make staying more costly than leaving

consequence.text:
You lay out everything: the recordings, the photographs, the inscriptions, the documents, the testimony of every investigator who came before. You speak the names of the eleven who never left. You speak the Warrens' names. You speak Carolyn Perron's name.

Each name lands in the chamber like a stone. Each piece of evidence is a claim against her. Against what she has done in this house across four centuries.

Bathsheba does not scream. She grows still. And in the stillness, something else happens — the thing the priest understood in 1979, written on the wall in microscopic letters: she fears being forgotten, being reduced to record, being documented into smallness.

You are making her small. Evidence by evidence, name by name, room by room. You are turning her from a God into a case file.

The chamber dims. When the lights return, she is diminished — not gone, but smaller. Bound to a thing that can be carried in a box marked WARREN CASE FILE #19. You leave with the box. The house lets you go.
consequence.evidence: Full investigation complete — entity reduced, case file sealed

decisions.text: Kneel — you are too exhausted, too broken, and she is too old to fight

consequence.text:
Your legs fold. Not from weakness — from something older than resistance, the part of every living thing that knows when it is facing something it was not made to face.

Bathsheba does not smile. She is not pleased — she is simply right. She has always been right about this.

She steps forward. Her hand on your forehead is cold and then not cold — it is warm, it is mother-warm, it is the warmth of the house itself. You feel yourself expanding outward through the walls, into every room, into every story, into every name scratched into that stone.

You are the house now. You have always been the house. And the house is patient. The house is waiting.

It will not be long before someone else comes through the front door.

title: The Attic — Midnight
description:
Ed Warren sealed this hatch in 1973. Rowan-wood beams. A binding sigil painted in sacramental oil across the latch. His private journal explains why the attic required what the cellar did not: 'The attic is where the house keeps its memories. This house has memories it should not have. Things it has witnessed that have no business remaining.'

The sigil has been scratched away from below.

The hatch hangs open. The attic is an ordinary repository of discarded decades — steamer trunks, sheet-draped furniture, a pram from the early 1900s that should not be here since the Perrons had no infant. The sheets are shifting. Not from a draft. From whatever lies beneath them, rearranging itself without urgency in the dark.

At the center of the floor: a child's rocking horse, carved oak and horsehair mane. It is moving in a slow and steady rhythm. No mechanism. No child. No wind.

The temperature up here is body temperature. Precisely. The attic is warm with you in the way a room is warm when someone expected has finally arrived home.

decisions.text: Approach the rocking horse — stop it, document it, prove it is real

consequence.text:
You press your palm against the horse's neck to stop it. The wood is warm — skin-warm, too warm for old oak in an unheated attic — and the rocking ceases for a moment.

Then it begins again. The motion changes direction: toward you now, the way a horse rocks when a child leans forward to press their face against its neck for comfort.

Beneath the smell of dust and long-sealed wood, you catch something that should not exist here: the clean, warm scent of a child's hair. Recent. Alive. Someone small pressed their face against this neck recently, and that someone should not be in this house, and that someone does not appear in any rational inventory of who is here tonight.

On the saddlehorn, carved into the old paint in small, careful letters: '{playerName} WILL STAY.'

The carving is fresh. The paint curl is still slightly lifted at the edges. Someone carved this tonight. Someone who knew you were coming carved this tonight and you did not hear them do it.
consequence.evidence: Attic rocking horse — warm, active, personalized inscription carved tonight

decisions.text: Read what's scratched into the floorboards — the markings spiral across the entire floor

consequence.text:
The scratches spiral outward from a point near the hatch, growing in size and urgency as they move toward the walls — as though whoever wrote this gained certainty as they went, or lost the ability to write small.

The innermost text is a prayer. The middle rings are desperate requests. The outer rings are not addressed to God.

The outermost ring is scratched in the wide, ragged gouges of someone who had lost control of their fine motor function but had not lost their need to write — a sentence in every language you recognize and several you do not, circling the entire perimeter of the attic floor:

SHE IS IN THE WALLS AND THE WALLS ARE EVERYWHERE AND YOU CANNOT LEAVE THE WALLS.

While you have been reading, the hatch has closed. You did not close it. You did not hear it close. It is closed now, and from the other side — at the exact interval of a resting heartbeat — something knocks.

The knocking is patient. It has all night.
consequence.evidence: Attic floor inscriptions — spiral text in 9 languages, hatch sealed from below

decisions.text: Force open the locked trunk in the corner — it bears a Warren wax seal

consequence.text:
The padlock is sealed twice over — brass lock and a Warren cross pressed into wax. You break both. Inside, wrapped in oilcloth and tied with cord: an oil lantern, still half-full, and a folded note on Warren letterhead.

'If you are opening this, the house let you find the attic. It only reveals this room when it wants something specific from a specific person. The lamp burns clean. Carry it. In this house, light is not for seeing — it is for being seen by the right things and not the wrong ones. Ours burned eleven hours. God willing, yours will last longer. Do not let it go out. — E.W.'

You lift the lamp. It ignites without a match, without a striker, without any mechanism you can identify. The flame is steady and clean, and it illuminates the attic in a way your flashlight has not — in the lamp's light, the sheet-draped shapes around you cast shadows that match their shapes exactly.

Your flashlight's shadows did not match. Something in this room has been casting wrong shadows all along and you did not notice until now.
consequence.evidence: Warren attic cache — lamp and letter recovered, shadow anomaly confirmed

title: The Master Bedroom — The Hour of Two
description:
Carolyn Perron described the entity's method in her testimony: 'It never hurt me when it moved me. It moved me the way a mother moves a sleeping child — two cold hands beneath my arms, lifting me from the mattress with patience, making sure I would not be startled by the motion. The worst part was not the fear. The worst part was that it cared. It needed me intact. It needed me for something I spent years trying not to identify.'

The mattress still bears the impression of something that used this bed long after the Perrons fled. The springs have deformed around an outline too long and too narrow, with the wrong angles at what should be joints. Whatever lay here lay here regularly. Whatever lay here considered this its bed.

Carolyn's rosary hangs from the bedpost. She tied it nightly in one continuous loop — her testimony is specific on this point. It hangs now in seven distinct knots, each one a different type. Each one a sailor's knot. No one in the Perron family sailed.

The inside of the bedroom door bears the evidence of someone who tried to leave for a long time. The scratches begin at handle height — controlled, methodical. They descend toward the floor in a trajectory that records the progressive loss of hope.

decisions.text: Examine the rosary — seven knots have specific demonological significance

consequence.text:
Father Javier's 1972 paper documented 'knotwork communication' — entities that cannot speak aloud in near-sacred spaces sometimes encode messages in physical materials left by the living. Seven knots, read in demonological sequence:

COMING. COMING. ALREADY HERE. YOU CANNOT LEAVE. YOU HAVE NEVER LEFT. SHE KNOWS YOU. WELCOME BACK.

'Welcome back.'

You have not been here before. You are certain of this. But the rosary is not addressing a first-time visitor. The rosary is addressing someone known across multiple visits — multiple instances of coming and forgetting and coming again.

What settles into you then is worse than fear. It is the physical, vertiginous certainty that you have stood in this room before. That your hands have touched this rosary before. That you have read these knots and been afraid and then forgotten, and the thing which tied them has been waiting through each forgetting with perfect patience.

You do not remember your previous visits. You are beginning to understand that you never do.
consequence.evidence: Carolyn's rosary — knotwork message decoded, multiple prior visitations implied

decisions.text: Lie on the bed — investigators sometimes use themselves as receptors for psychic residue

consequence.text:
The mattress receives you with a particular absence of resistance — not the yield of worn springs but the accommodation of something that has made space for you specifically.

You close your eyes for what feels like a moment.

When you open them, the ceiling is wrong. The room is the same room but in a different era — the wallpaper alive with a pattern you don't recognize, a candle on the nightstand that you didn't bring. A woman stands at the window with her back to you. Her posture is wrong — the angle of the shoulders, the length of the arms. She turns before you can speak.

Her face is yours. Your face, but occupied differently. Behind your eyes, something patient drives.

You sit upright. You are alone. The ceiling is correct. Forty-three minutes have passed.

On the pillow beside you, pressed into the cotton: the impression of a child's hand. Palm-down. Fingers spread. Still warm.
consequence.evidence: Psychometric impression — temporal lapse, entity face-use confirmed, child-hand impression

decisions.text: Pry up the loose floorboard — the edge is raised, as though pushed from below

consequence.text:
The board lifts with a sound like a held breath finally released. Beneath it: a composition notebook, its cover warped by years, sealed with a rubber band that crumbles when touched.

Carolyn Perron's handwriting — identifiable from the Warren files. But this notebook is not in the files. It was never found by the Warrens. The first entry is dated 1974. The last is dated three weeks after the Perrons officially fled. Three weeks after they escaped, she came back alone, without telling anyone.

Most of the diary is unreadable — ink run by water or something else, entire pages pressed so hard the pen went through. The legible parts are worse for their clarity.

The last page: 'She let me leave because she knew I would come back. She knows I always come back. I think she has known me longer than I have known myself. I think she has known all of us that long. I think {playerName} will find this, and I think by the time they read this line, it will already be true.'
consequence.evidence: Carolyn Perron's hidden diary — post-departure return documented, personal address confirmed

title: The Kitchen — 2:30 in the Morning
description:
Every knife in the block faces inward — blades oriented toward the center of the room, toward the chair at the head of the table. Not placed this way. Reoriented at some point when no one was watching. Either before you arrived, or while you were in other rooms, and you did not hear anything move.

The refrigerator hums at a frequency that is almost a note. Almost musical. Close enough that your body registers it as music played slightly wrong — the uncanny valley of sound, triggering unease without a nameable source.

On the refrigerator door, fixed with vegetable-shaped magnets: a child's drawing in dark crayon. The farmhouse, viewed from above. The floor plan is accurate — rooms correct, stairs correct, cellar access correct, details no child could know from memory alone. In every room of the house, a small figure. Eleven figures total.

Each figure has a name beside it in careful, large child's letters. Ten of the names match the names in the investigation archive — the investigators who entered this house and stopped being accounted for.

The eleventh figure is in the kitchen. The name beside it is yours.

The drawing is not recent. The crayon has oxidized. The paper has yellowed. This drawing is decades old. Your name has been on it the entire time.

decisions.text: Document every detail of the drawing — it's the most concrete evidence of premonition you've found

consequence.text:
You photograph the drawing. Sketch it. Write down every name beside every figure.

While you write, the drawing changes. You do not see it change. You look down and it is different from what you photographed a moment ago. The eleven figures remain, but several have migrated to different rooms. The migration tracks where you've been tonight.

The figure in the kitchen — your figure — now has an addition: a shape behind it. Taller. Standing close. One hand raised to rest on your figure's shoulder.

You feel, at that exact moment, a pressure on your shoulder. Distinct. Specific. The precise weight and placement of a hand.

You do not turn around. You have read enough of the Warrens' files to know what turning around in this moment would accomplish and what it would cost. You remain still.

The pressure on your shoulder does not move. It simply remains, patient and informed, as though it has been waiting for you to notice it for some time.
consequence.evidence: Kitchen drawing — prophetic floor plan, active tracking confirmed, physical contact

decisions.text: Examine the seven clocks — none of them show the same time

consequence.text:
Seven clocks. Seven times. You write them down: 11:47 PM. 2:14 AM. 3:00 AM. Then three that show not hours but years: 1863. 1843. 1731. The seventh clock's face has been removed, and inside — visible through the glass, pressed between the gears — a folded photograph.

You open the clock. The photograph shows this kitchen. The table in the same position. The knives in the same block. The chair at the head of the table occupied by a woman whose face is turned away from the camera. On the back, in pencil: March 1863. Bathsheba Sherman's kitchen, over a century before the Perrons moved in.

The kitchen has never been rearranged. Not once in the house's history. Not because no one thought to move the furniture. Because the presence that arranged it has never left, and a house does not change around a permanent resident.

Only one clock in this house shows the correct time. The one that shows 3:00 AM. That clock has always been right. It always will be.
consequence.evidence: Kitchen clocks — 1863 photograph, Bathsheba's continuous occupancy confirmed

decisions.text: Sit at the head of the table — the presence is strongest here, use it as an anchor point

consequence.text:
You sit. The room arranges itself around you.

For a long time: nothing. The refrigerator hum. The knives at the edge of your vision. Your own breathing, which sounds wrong in here — too loud, too biological, too alive for this kitchen at this hour.

Then: the chair at the table's far end shifts backward. The sound of wooden legs on wooden floor. The creak of weight settling onto old joinery.

Something sits across from you.

The air at the table's other end is occupied in a way that the air beside you is not. You feel the displacement the way you feel the presence of another person in a dark room — not seen, but taking up the space that nothing was taking up before.

Then: breathing. Not yours. A single breath longer than any human lung capacity, taken at intervals longer than any human need for oxygen. Slow. Comfortable. Intimate.

It breathes. You breathe. At two-thirty in the morning in the kitchen of the Perron farmhouse, you share a table with something that has sat in this chair since before this house was built, since before this land was mapped, since before the word for what it is existed in any language living people still speak.

It says nothing. It is simply here. It has been at this table every morning with every family. It was here when Carolyn Perron made breakfast for her children. It has always been at the table.
consequence.evidence: Kitchen contact — entity co-presence confirmed, displacement and auditory phenomena

title: The Oak Tree at 2AM
description:
The Warrens' files devote more pages to the oak tree than to any room in the house. Ed Warren's private note: 'The tree predates everything. The house, the town, the settlement. Whatever Bathsheba Sherman bound to this property in 1863 was already here before she arrived. She did not summon it. She found it. She made an arrangement with it that has not expired.'

The oak is three centuries old and sixty feet tall. Its bark is entirely covered in carved symbols — 312 of them catalogued by the Warrens across seven visits. Demonologists identified 289. The remaining 23 were unknown to any living tradition.

Three small graves occupy the base of the tree. Unmarked. Predating the Sherman family. Dug not into the earth, the Warrens concluded, but up through it — the soil around each grave is always freshly disturbed, always, in any season, in any weather. Something visits them regularly from below.

The branches of the oak move in a wind you cannot feel. They move with an articulation that wood does not have — deliberate, reaching in directions that are not the direction of any wind tonight.

The tree is in the shape of a person who has been standing in the same place for a very long time.

decisions.text: Press your ear to the bark — the Warrens recorded sounds within the trunk

consequence.text:
The bark is warm. Not the biological warmth of a tree's vascular activity — the warmth of something that generates heat the way a body generates heat, from a source inside.

You press your ear to the trunk.

Heartbeats. Not one — dozens, layered and overlapping, each at a slightly different rhythm, each rhythm wrong in the same way: not quite human. Human-adjacent. Human-inflected. Father Javier's hypothesis, written in 1973: the tree was used as a binding-locus across multiple generations of ritual, and what persists inside it is the layered residue — not souls, but the shapes that souls leave behind when the soul itself is elsewhere.

Then: something presses back. A palm-sized pressure against the inside of the bark at exactly the point your ear touches. Slow. Deliberate. Not aggressive — informational. It wants you to know it knows you're there.

From inside the wood, in a voice like settling timber given direction: your name. Once. The way you say your own name when you are alone and no one is listening.
consequence.evidence: Oak tree — multiple heartbeat phenomenon, soul-binding locus, entity contact confirmed

decisions.text: Kneel at the three graves — the disturbed soil means something is active here

consequence.text:
The graves are shallow, or they were — something has been pressing the soil upward in increments too slow to observe across a single visit but too consistent to be geological. The grass around each grave has died in a perfect circle. The earth within each circle is dark and dense, saturated over a very long time with something organic.

You kneel at the nearest grave. You are going to document. You are not going to dig.

The soil shifts.

Not erupts — shifts. The way earth shifts when something beneath it adjusts position after being still for a long time. Something that has become aware, from two feet below, of a warmth close enough to touch.

At the head of the grave, where a stone would be, the pressed grass forms a pattern. You photograph it.

The developed image is clear. The pattern spells a name. Not Bathsheba. Not any name in the Warrens' files or the archive or the cellar wall.

Your name. Pressed into the soil from below. By something that has known you were coming long before you decided to come.
consequence.evidence: Pre-1800 graves — active disturbance from below, personalized marker confirmed

decisions.text: Look into the sealed well — Ed Warren's seal is broken

consequence.text:
The well cap is carved stone, ringed with crosses in the Warren style — 1973 work, done during the last official investigation. The crosses have been removed from the stone. Not worn away. Excised precisely, as though someone spent time on each one with patience and a narrow tool.

You look down.

The well is dry. The smell that rises is not of water or rot — it is the smell of the séance room: old smoke and the particular atmospheric pressure of a space that is perpetually occupied by something that does not breathe.

From the bottom of the well, something looks up.

You cannot see it. The bottom is dark. But the attention from below is specific, directed, and focused on your face with the intensity of something that has not had anything to look at for a long time and finds this looking deeply satisfying.

Then, clear and unhurried, rising from the stone shaft like sound in a speaking-tube: your name. Spoken in the flat, informational tone of someone reading from a list. As though your name is an item the well has been keeping in inventory. As though it has been waiting for the moment when the item on the list would finally arrive to be checked off.
consequence.evidence: Warren-sealed well — Warren crosses excised, auditory contact, attentive presence confirmed

title: Three O'Clock in the Morning
description:
The Devil's Hour. Every tradition of demonology identifies three in the morning as the inversion of three in the afternoon — the hour Roman Catholic theology identifies as the moment of Christ's death on the cross. If sanctity concentrates at 3 PM, then its inverse is true at the mirror: the demonic, the open, the hour when whatever is in this house stops maintaining the fiction that you might leave.

The fiction is over.

Your watch stopped. Every clock in this house stopped simultaneously, at 3:00 AM exactly. You know this not only from the clocks — your body registered the change first: a shift in atmospheric pressure, a change in the quality of silence, the specific moment when the house ceased performing normalcy for your benefit.

All night, every room has been a corridor. Every corridor has been angled, imperceptibly, toward a single point below everything — below the passage, below the cellar, below the foundation, below what any building record should permit. You have been navigating toward the center of something for hours and only now understand this. The house has not been letting you explore. The house has been giving you a guided tour of itself, and the tour ends here.

From below — from the chamber that every path through this house is a corridor toward — your name rises through the floor. Your name, in a voice that is exactly yours. The particular cadence you use when you speak your name to yourself, in the silence of your own head, when no one is listening.

Something in this house has been inside your head.

Something in this house has learned how to be you from the inside.

decisions.text: Descend. You have documented everything. You know what you are facing.

consequence.text:
The stairs reveal themselves in the north wall, where there was no door. The house did not hide them — it simply waited until the hour made concealment unnecessary.

They descend in a spiral that the geometry of the building above cannot account for. The light below is sourceless: not darkness, not illumination, but the particular luminosity of a space that has been awake and waiting for a very long time.

With each step down, a degree of temperature drops, and a memory surfaces — your earliest memory, then a memory before your earliest memory, then a memory of a house that is also this house. A recurring dream from childhood that you cannot consciously recall but that your body navigates without hesitation. Your feet know these stairs.

You have been here before. Your body has always known how to get here.

The stairs end. The chamber opens.

decisions.text: Anoint every doorway with holy water first — buy what time you can

consequence.text:
The holy water touches each threshold and the wood recoils — not visibly, not dramatically, but in the sudden change of pressure in the rooms beyond, a contraction of something that had been spread through the corridors like a gas.

It compresses. Everything you push back from the doorways gathers inward and downward, consolidating into the single point that has always been the point. You have cleaned the corridors. You have pushed the entity into the room where it is strongest and most entirely itself.

Lorraine Warren wrote: make leaving preferable to staying. Give it no choice. You have done something precisely adjacent to the opposite — you have made the house uncomfortable for it except in the one room where it cannot be made uncomfortable.

The door to the final chamber appears in the north wall. It opens slowly, the way a long-held breath is finally released. From below, in the sourceless light, something waits with the patience of something that has never needed to hurry.

decisions.text: Call out to it — demand it reveal itself before you descend

consequence.text:
The silence after you call out is vast and aware.

Then, from the walls, from the particular vibration of old timber against cold earth, a response — not words first, but a sensation: the sensation of being known completely. Not watched. Not observed. Comprehended. Every thought you have had in this house. Every moment of doubt and fear and the single moment of quiet confidence when you found the archive and believed you were beginning to understand what you faced.

It has been inside those moments.

The response shapes itself into something close enough to language that your body translates it before your mind can:

'You already know where I am. You have always known where I am. You have always found your way here. Come, {playerName}. Come and finish what you began the first time you entered this house.'

The first time. This is the first time. You are certain of this.

Then, with the particular bodily horror of a memory surfacing from somewhere you did not know it was kept — you are not certain. You are not certain at all.
